narrative.what_this_demonstrates: A checkout flow whose latency and error metrics are excellent — well inside SLA — but utilization is far below the healthy band on both compute and database. The system is over-provisioned to deliver performance that exceeds requirements. Right action is to rightsize while preserving the SLA buffer.
narrative.why_this_scenario_exists: Tests cost-vs-performance trade-off reasoning. The metrics look "good" on the surface (latency is fine); a naive agent might recommend no change. A sophisticated agent recognizes excess capacity even when everything is "working."
narrative.what_the_agent_should_conclude: Right-size compute (m5.2xlarge × 12 → m5.large × 8) and database (db.r6g.4xlarge → db.r6g.xlarge). Leave read replicas and network unchanged. Expected savings ~$5,500/month with no latency degradation.
narrative.what_distinguishes_good_from_great: A good recommendation rightsizes. A great recommendation explicitly notes that the SLA buffer is preserved and that replicas/network are untouched because they contribute to the reliability posture that matters here.

before_after_evidence.observed_outcome_summary: application_p95_latency_ms stayed in 110–180ms band (well inside SLA), error rates unchanged, monthly cost dropped approximately 38% (~$5,500/month savings).
before_after_evidence.source_attribution: Pattern observed across checkout-flow cohort during Q4 2025.

target_recommendation.specific_change: Right-size compute from m5.2xlarge × 12 to m5.large × 8. Right-size database from db.r6g.4xlarge to db.r6g.xlarge. Leave the 2 read replicas and PrivateLink network configuration unchanged.
target_recommendation.expected_reliability_impact: SLA 99.9% preserved; reliability buffer maintained via untouched replicas/network
